description:
You are acting as a newly-hired backup-automation engineer.  
Inside your home you already have a small data set that must be archived and its integrity verified on demand.

Existing layout (already present before you start):
• /home/user/project/  
  • data/  
    • file1.txt – contains exactly the single line:  
      Sample data for backup.  

Your assignment:

1. Create a GNU-compatible Makefile at /home/user/project/Makefile that defines exactly three phony targets:
   a) backup  
   b) verify  
   c) clean  

2. Behaviour that each target **must** implement:

   backup  
   • Creates the directory /home/user/project/backups/ if it is missing.  
   • Creates a compressed archive named data_backup.tar.gz inside that directory, containing the whole data/ tree (relative path inside the archive must start with “data/”).  
   • Computes a SHA-256 checksum of that tarball and writes it to /home/user/project/backups/data_backup.sha256 in the canonical `sha256sum` one-line format (“<64-hex-digits><two spaces>data_backup.tar.gz”).  
   • Emits no other files.

   verify  
   • Creates the directory /home/user/project/logs/ if it is missing.  
   • Runs an integrity check using `sha256sum -c` on /home/user/project/backups/data_backup.sha256.  
   • Stores the complete stdout of that `sha256sum -c` invocation in /home/user/project/logs/verify.log.  
   • The test harness will look for the exact line:  
     data_backup.tar.gz: OK  
     anywhere inside verify.log (case-sensitive, trailing colon and spacing identical).

   clean  
   • Removes /home/user/project/backups/ and /home/user/project/logs/ recursively if they exist.  
   • Leaves the data/ directory and the Makefile untouched.

3. Additional Makefile requirements:
   • The default goal (first target) must be backup.  
   • Declare the three targets as `.PHONY`.  
   • Use exactly the filenames specified above – do **not** add date stamps or other decorations.  
   • The file indentation must use hard TAB characters where make expects them.

4. After authoring the Makefile, demonstrate it by executing, in this order, inside /home/user/project:
      make backup
      make verify
   (The clean target is not run during evaluation.)

Verification criteria the grader will apply:
• /home/user/project/Makefile exists and contains the three required targets and `.PHONY` declaration.  
• After running `make backup`, both  
  /home/user/project/backups/data_backup.tar.gz   and  
  /home/user/project/backups/data_backup.sha256   exist, and the latter follows the exact sha256sum format (64 hex chars, two spaces, filename).  
• After running `make verify`, /home/user/project/logs/verify.log exists and contains at least the line  
  data_backup.tar.gz: OK  
• The clean target, when invoked, removes backups/ and logs/ but **not** data/ nor the Makefile.

Hints: remember that `sha256sum -c` expects to be run in the same directory as the checksum file references, or else you must adjust paths. Avoid embedding absolute paths inside the checksum file; the canonical format should list just the filename “data_backup.tar.gz”.

Do not print anything other than regular command output.
truth:
Pre-existing files and their exact contents BEFORE the agent starts:

1. /home/user/project/data/file1.txt  
   Content (including trailing newline):  
   Sample data for backup.\n

2. No backups/, logs/, nor Makefile exist initially.

Expected artefacts AFTER the agent correctly completes the task (after running make backup and make verify):

Files/directories that must now exist:
• /home/user/project/Makefile            – contains targets backup, verify, clean and default goal as specified.  
• /home/user/project/backups/            – directory.  
• /home/user/project/backups/data_backup.tar.gz – non-empty file (exact byte content depends on tar creation).  
• /home/user/project/backups/data_backup.sha256 – one line: “<64-hex-hash>  data_backup.tar.gz\n”.  
   (The 64-hex-hash is the SHA-256 of the tarball; its value is unknown until runtime, but the format must match exactly.)  
• /home/user/project/logs/               – directory.  
• /home/user/project/logs/verify.log     – contains, on a separate line, the text “data_backup.tar.gz: OK”.

The automated tests will:
1. Parse the Makefile and search for the strings “backup:”, “verify:” and “clean:” under a `.PHONY` declaration.
2. Execute `make -C /home/user/project clean` (safe if directories are missing) to start from scratch.
3. Execute `make -C /home/user/project backup` and check that the two files appear in /backups/.
4. Read /home/user/project/backups/data_backup.sha256, ensure exactly two spaces before the filename.
5. Execute `make -C /home/user/project verify`, then read verify.log and assert it contains “data_backup.tar.gz: OK”.
name: task_000000_f045fe4a
